questions.question: Explain the working of Insertion Sort with an example array [5, 2, 4, 6, 1, 3]. Show how it builds the sorted portion.
keyPoints: Start from second element (2), compare with 5, shift 5 right, insert 2 at position 0
Take 4, compare with 5 and 2, shift 5 and 4, insert 4 at position 1
Continue for remaining elements until entire array is sorted
Final sorted array: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

questions.question: Why is Insertion Sort preferred for nearly sorted arrays? Analyze its time complexity for such cases.
keyPoints: In nearly sorted array, each element needs to move only a few positions
Time complexity becomes O(n) when array is almost sorted
Minimal number of shifts and comparisons required
Adaptive behavior makes it efficient for incremental sorting

questions.question: Describe the exact steps of Selection Sort on the array [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]. How many passes and swaps are required?
keyPoints: Pass 1: Find minimum (11), swap with 64 -> [11, 25, 12, 22, 64]
Pass 2: Find min in remaining (12), swap with 25 -> [11, 12, 25, 22, 64]
Pass 3: Find min in remaining (22), swap with 25 -> [11, 12, 22, 25, 64]
Pass 4: Find min in remaining (25), already in place -> Total 4 passes, 4 swaps

questions.question: What is stability in sorting algorithms? Explain which of the three algorithms (Insertion, Selection, Bubble) are stable and why.
keyPoints: Stable sorting preserves relative order of equal elements
Insertion Sort is stable - only shifts elements greater than key
Selection Sort is unstable - swapping may change order of equals
Bubble Sort is stable - only swaps adjacent different elements

questions.question: Write pseudo-code for Bubble Sort with the optimization that stops early if no swaps occur in a pass.
keyPoints: Initialize swapped = true, n = array length
While swapped is true: set swapped = false, loop i from 0 to n-2
Compare arr[i] and arr[i+1], if wrong order swap and set swapped = true
If no swap occurred in complete pass, break - array is sorted

questions.question: Compare the three sorting algorithms (Insertion, Selection, Bubble) based on: (a) Time complexity (b) Space complexity (c) Adaptive nature (d) Stability.
keyPoints: Time: Insertion/Selection/Bubble all O(n²) average/worst; Insertion/Bubble O(n) best
Space: All three are O(1) in-place algorithms
Adaptive: Insertion and optimized Bubble are adaptive; Selection is not
Stability: Insertion and Bubble stable; Selection unstable

questions.question: When is Selection Sort preferred over Insertion Sort? Give specific scenarios with justification.
keyPoints: Selection Sort preferred when memory writes are costly (exactly n-1 writes)
Useful when array size is small and constant overhead matters
When stability is not required but minimum swaps are important
Worst-case write-heavy scenarios where swap cost is minimal compared to shift

questions.question: Analyze and trace through each pass of Bubble Sort on the array [5, 1, 4, 2, 8]. Also explain how the optimization flag improves performance. What is the final sorted array after each pass?
keyPoints: Pass 1: Compare adjacent pairs, swap if needed: 5>1 swap, 5>4 swap, 5>2 swap, 5>8 no swap -> [1, 4, 2, 5, 8]
Pass 2: 1<4 no swap, 4>2 swap, 4<5 no swap -> [1, 2, 4, 5, 8]
Pass 3: 1<2 no swap, 2<4 no swap -> already sorted
Optimization: Use flag, detect sorted after pass 2, terminate early. Best case becomes O(n). Final: [1, 2, 4, 5, 8]

questions.question: Derive the time complexity for Insertion Sort in best, average, and worst cases. Explain with mathematical analysis why Insertion Sort is considered better than other O(n²) algorithms.
keyPoints: Best case: Already sorted - only n-1 comparisons, T(n) = O(n)
Worst case: Reverse sorted - Σ(i-1) = n(n-1)/2 comparisons, T(n) = O(n²)
Average case: Random order - roughly half inversions, T(n) = O(n²)
Better because: Adaptive (best case O(n)), stable, in-place, less movement than selection, efficient for online/small data

questions.question: Explain why Selection Sort is not considered a 'divide and conquer' algorithm despite having two sub-problems in each iteration. Also justify if Selection Sort can ever be preferred in practical applications.
keyPoints: Divide and conquer splits problem, conquers recursively, combines results. Selection Sort doesn't divide logically - it selects minimum from unsorted portion
Not truly recursive in standard implementation
Preferred when: write operations are expensive (memory writes), stability not needed, deterministic behavior required regardless of input
Small datasets where O(n²) overhead is comparable to O(n log n), but requires exactly n-1 swaps always

questions.question: Write complete C program to implement Selection Sort. Analyze its time and space complexity. Also modify the program to make it stable while maintaining similar efficiency.
keyPoints: Standard selection sort finds min in unsorted portion, swaps with first unsorted position
Time: O(n²) for all cases, Space: O(1)
For stable version: Instead of swapping, shift all elements greater than min one position right, then place min at correct position
This increases writes but maintains stability, still O(n²) time but slightly more operations
